text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a hemion hernia. It's a appendicitis within an inguinal hernia. This is a elderly gentleman with a lot of medical problems on anticoagulation who had developed appendicitis a few weeks earlier and was treated with antibiotics by his physicians due to risk of surgery. He's been optimized a bit and he was really complaining of tremendous groin pain and he just didn't think that he could wait. On exam, you could feel an irreducible mass in the groin and CAT scan did confirm that it was the appendix. Because of his ongoing symptoms and him being better optimized, it was recommended that he go to the operating room for an appendectomy.

The surgeon identifies adhesions between the cecum and appendix within the hernia sac. Using surgical instruments, the surgeon grasps the mesoappendix and begins dividing the attachments to free the appendix from the indirect space.

I was not planning on doing a hernia repair at the time of this operation. This was more or less just to get the appendix out. I chose an MIS operation because I knew that it would take me fifteen minutes or so and avoid dragging the appendix through the groin if we were to have done this in an open fashion. I have subsequently repaired this gentleman's inguinal hernia. I did an open lichtenstein under sedation. Their medical comorbidities really were prohibitive and We wanted to avoid a second general anesthesia.

The surgeon has mostly extracted the appendix from the indirect inguinal space and is carefully dissecting additional adhesions to expose the appendiceal mesentery. A window is created in the mesentery to facilitate the insertion of a ligature, allowing for the removal of the remaining meso-appendix.

The surgeon addresses residual adhesions around the appendix to create a clear base for ligation. An endo loop is placed at the base of the appendix, followed by a second loop, before proceeding to divide the appendix.
The surgeon cauterizes the exposed mucosa of the appendix stump, leaving it as it is without imbrication.
